questions.question: DQN replaces the Q-table with:
options: A lookup dictionary
A neural network that takes state as input and outputs Q-values for all actions
questions.explanation: NN(state) -> [Q(s,a1), Q(s,a2), ...]. Enables Q-learning for continuous state spaces.

questions.question: Experience Replay in DQN:
options: Is optional
Stores (s,a,r,s') transitions in a replay buffer and samples mini-batches for training
questions.explanation: Breaks correlation between consecutive samples. Stabilises training significantly.

questions.question: The Target Network in DQN:
options: Is the same as the main network
A periodically updated copy of the main network used for computing target Q-values -- stabilises training
questions.explanation: Without target network, the Q-value targets shift every step causing oscillation.

questions.question: DQN is most suitable for:
options: All RL problems
Discrete action spaces with high-dimensional state observations (e.g. image-based Atari games)
questions.explanation: DQN mastered 49 Atari games from raw pixels -- a landmark result (DeepMind, 2015).

questions.question: Double DQN addresses:
options: Exploration
Overestimation of Q-values -- uses main network to select action, target network to evaluate it
questions.explanation: Standard DQN consistently overestimates Q-values due to max operator bias.

questions.question: Dueling DQN separates:
options: Policy and value
The state value V(s) and the advantage A(s,a) -- better generalisation across actions
questions.explanation: Q(s,a) = V(s) + A(s,a). The value stream learns which states are good regardless of action.

questions.question: Prioritised Experience Replay samples:
options: Random transitions equally
Transitions with higher TD error more frequently -- focuses on harder-to-learn transitions
questions.explanation: Important experiences are replayed more often, improving sample efficiency.

questions.question: The main limitation of DQN:
options: Too slow
Only handles discrete action spaces -- cannot directly apply to continuous action domains like robotics
questions.explanation: For continuous actions, use DDPG, TD3, or SAC instead of DQN.

questions.question: Batch size in DQN training affects:
options: Only memory
Learning stability -- larger batches reduce gradient variance but require more memory
questions.explanation: Typical DQN batch size: 32-64 samples from the replay buffer.

questions.question: Rainbow DQN combines:
options: Only DQN and DDPG
Six improvements: Double DQN + Dueling + Prioritised Replay + Multi-step + Noisy + Distributional
questions.explanation: The combination significantly outperforms each individual improvement on Atari benchmarks.
